invariant_definition: The Lord as governing consciousness: the principle that rules, orders, and dispenses. In Sanskrit philosophical use, *Īśvara* does more work than the English "God." It is the term around which disputes about causality, providence, the fruits of *karma*, worship, and scriptural authority are organized. Vedānta asks whether *Īśvara* is merely the efficient cause or both efficient and material cause of the world, whether *Īśvara* is the highest description of reality or a conditioned one, and how the Lord stands to *māyā*, *prakṛti*, and bound selves. Yoga uses the term differently again: there *Īśvara* is a special *puruṣa*, untouched by affliction and karmic residue, and functions as a meditative and salvific ideal without needing the full Vedāntic creator-theology. The invariant core is therefore rule, sovereignty, and spiritual authority; the surrounding metaphysical commitments differ sharply. Any translation has to keep that causal and soteriological structure in view.

per_school.definition: Īśvara is the omniscient manifestation of Brahman as reflected in māyā, the sattva-śuddha side of prakṛti brought under control; lordship here is thus articulated through a pratibimba-upādhi structure rather than through any alteration in Brahman [[Pañc. 1.15-16](cite://vidyaranya/panchadashi/1.15-16)]. Sarvajñātman accordingly describes the cosmic Lord as the tamaḥ-pratibimba of the paramātman entering relation with the world, just as the buddhi-located reflection of consciousness becomes the kartṛ of karma [[SS I.327](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327)].

Īśvara is the eka-pradhāna-puruṣa whose niyamana over the sentient and non-sentient whole is read from the world's ekecchānuvidhāna and from the dependence of all embodied agency upon one ruler [[ĪS 16-18](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/16-18)]. Deśika formulates the same siddhānta through śarīrātma-bhāva: cit and acit stand to Īśvara as body to indwelling self, so that dhāryatva, niyāmyatva, and śeṣatva are constitutive of their relation to him [[RTS 104](cite://vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/104)] [[RTS 104-105](cite://vedanta-desika/rahasya-traya-sara/104–105)].

Īśvara is Viṣṇu as the unique svatantra, and the first articulation of the world's order is the real, beginningless jīveśvara-bheda that anchors the bhedapañcaka [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. His lordship is not exhausted by cosmic origination, since even the liberated remain tadvaśa and Viṣṇu alone is their superior support and adhipati [[VTV 3.25](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.25)]. Madhva also glosses the Gītā's uttama-puruṣa as the avyaya īśvara who enters and sustains the three worlds, making sovereignty a matter of real ontological supremacy [[Gītā 15.17](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.17)].

Jīva Gosvāmī construes the Lord-jīva relation through acintya-śakti: the abheda-śāstras are to be coordinated only after one has preserved both difference and non-difference, the jīva standing to Bhagavān as ray-atoms to the sun [[Paramātma-S. §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Because Bhagavān is the māyā-āśraya while the jīva is māyā-mohita, the established bheda grounds bhajana to him alone as the school's abhidheya [[Paramātma-S. §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)]. Īśvara in this register is therefore the Bhagavān who is sarvādhika-guṇa-śālin and the sole object of parama-prema, the prayojana of the whole system [[Paramātma-S. §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

The Nimbārka line places Īśvara within a triad of coeval reals, since the jīva is jñāna-svarūpa yet hareḥ adhīna and therefore defined by intrinsic dependence upon the Lord [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. That Lord is the supreme Brahman Kṛṣṇa, aśeṣa-kalyāṇa-guṇa-eka-rāśi and the proper object of meditation, so the school's aṃśa-aṃśin structure is theistic through and through [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)]. The jīva's bondage and release are then governed by beginningless māyā and bhagavat-prasāda, without canceling the plurality of distinctions internal to the Lord's order [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)].

In Śuddhādvaita the Lord is Puruṣottama-Kṛṣṇa, the very Brahman that is samavāyi-kāraṇa, pervading all effects as sat-cit-ānanda while nāma and rūpa belong to the kārya-side [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. Vallabha's causal theology is avikṛta-pariṇāma: Brahman acts as its own kartṛ and yet manifests the world without surrendering its unmodified plenitude [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. The jīva is therefore Brahman's real aṃśa, and attainment of the Lord is completed not by bare brahmajñāna alone but through bhakti in the mode of divine varaṇa and Puruṣottama-prāpti [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)] [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Yoga defines Īśvara as a puruṣa-viśeṣa untouched by kleśa, karma, vipāka, and āśaya, so lordship here names unsurpassed purity rather than an account of material causation [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. Accordingly, īśvara-praṇidhāna is treated as a samādhi-bhāvanā-viśeṣa that serves directly as a cause of asaṃprajñāta-samādhi [[YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23)].

translator_note: "God" is often too blunt. It imports Abrahamic assumptions about creation, personality, and uniqueness that fit some uses of *Īśvara* but not others, especially Yoga and Advaita. "The Lord" is usually safer because it preserves the rulership sense and leaves open the school-specific metaphysics.
